paragraphs.text: NERA ECONOMICS EXPERTS EXPERIENCE

section_path: Priorities for the Digital Fairness Act from an economist's perspective
paragraphs.text: A submission to the EC call for evidence

section_path: Priorities for the Digital Fairness Act from an economist's perspective
paragraphs.text: 24 October 2025

section_path: Priorities for the Digital Fairness Act from an economist's perspective
paragraphs.text: Introduction & Key Comments

section_path: 1. Introduction & Key Comments
paragraphs.text: The European Commission has launched a public consultation and Call for Evidence on the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA), an initiative to strengthen consumer protection in the digital environment by addressing manipulative and misleading practices online. This note will look at some of the challenges, how these can be resolved from an economic perspective and provides case experience from the US where two of the authors have dealt with the economic evidence to be used in regulatory review of pricing practices and cancelation practices, both from the perspective of a regulator (the FTC) and affected companies.

section_path: 1. Introduction & Key Comments
paragraphs.text: A key challenge in developing effective consumer policy enforcement is to provide regulators, consumers and companies with clear and robust rules on:

section_path: 1. Introduction & Key Comments
paragraphs.text: Defining what is truly abusive ("unfair") behaviour and what is legitimate customer retention / product design that some (but far from all) customers find annoying and which does not require regulation.

section_path: 1. Introduction & Key Comments
paragraphs.text: Recognising the cost of intervention, e.g. that banning practices that currently "cross-subsidise" prices or generate revenue may change pricing structures, features, and product choices, with potentially negative pass-through to consumers.

section_path: 1. Introduction & Key Comments
paragraphs.text: The Commission has carried out a "back of the envelope" calculation quantifying financial consumer detriment for EU consumers from problematic practices by traders online at EUR 7.9 bn in 2023, based on the following formula:

section_path: 1. Introduction & Key Comments
paragraphs.text: $$ \text{Financial Detriment}_{\text{Year}} = (\text{Total Number of Consumers}) \times (\text{Percentage of all consumers with last internet online purchase in the last 3 months}_{\text{Year}}) \times (\text{Complaint's incident rate}_{\text{Year}}) \times (\text{Individual Consumer Detriment}_{\text{Year}}), $$

section_path: 1. Introduction & Key Comments
paragraphs.text: where the individual consumer detriment is calculated as

section_path: 1. Introduction & Key Comments
paragraphs.text: $$ \text{Individual Consumer Detriment} = \text{Extra charges as result of problem} + \text{Costs of repairs or replacement at your own expense} + \text{costs of dispute resolution} + \text{costs of expert advice} + \text{extra costs} - \text{Reimbursement at original price paid for products.}^1 $$

section_path: 1. Introduction & Key Comments
paragraphs.text: These calculations are a start, but they are rough at best. They provide evidence for the existence of costs relating to unfair online commercial practices both on an individual and on a macroeconomic level. But they do not capture the overall economic welfare effects of these practices and of proposed measures to address them. For a start, the Commission highlights but does not quantify the positive welfare effects of unproblematic online commercial practices to consumers. Assessing the overall welfare effects of tighter future consumer protection may make this necessary, given the currently blurry line between "problematic" and "unproblematic" practices creating a risk of "false positives" where practices that may be disliked but do serve legitimate market purposes could be mistakenly labeled as abusive or unfair.. Moreover, novel consumer

section_path: 1. Introduction & Key Comments
paragraphs.text: © NERA

section_path: 1. Introduction & Key Comments
paragraphs.text: For all components of individual financial detriment, the Commission used median values from responses to a consumer survey carried out as part of the EU Digital Fairness Fitness Check. For further detail on the Commission’s calculation of financial consumer detriment and relevant data sources, see EC (03.10.2024): Fitness Check of EU Consumer Law on Digital Fairness, Section 3.

section_path: 1. Introduction & Key Comments
paragraphs.text: EC (03.10.2024): Fitness Check of EU Consumer Law on Digital Fairness, Section 3.

section_path: 1. Introduction & Key Comments
paragraphs.text: Assessing Consumer Harm in a digital world

section_path: 1. Introduction & Key Comments
paragraphs.text: legislation may alter business compliance costs, which will either have to be carried by producers or consumers and affect supply and / or demand in turn. These overall effects on demand and supply subsequently determine the overall effect on consumer welfare.

section_path: 1. Introduction & Key Comments
paragraphs.text: These considerations highlight the challenge of robust quantification in this area. A credible impact assessment should therefore examine net welfare effects, including consumer harm reduction, business compliance costs and market dynamics, drawing on empirical evidence across different categories of digital services

section_path: 2. Assessing Consumer Harm in a digital world
paragraphs.text: As part of its 2024 "Digital Fairness Fitness Check", the European Commission analysed whether the existing EU consumer protection legislation is still relevant, effective, and efficient, in the context of new digital challenges. It sourced information from surveys of consumers, businesses, and other stakeholders, from calls for evidence and a public consultations, interviews, and case studies.

section_path: 2. Assessing Consumer Harm in a digital world
paragraphs.text: The "Fitness Check" concluded that the core directives do not fully address evolving online harms. It further highlighted the need for targeted updates, clearer rules, and stronger enforcement in the digital context. The Commission identified the following phenomena as problems to be potentially addressed by future European consumer protection legislation on digital matters (the Digital Fairness Act), which are also reflected in the Call for Evidence:

section_path: 2. Assessing Consumer Harm in a digital world
paragraphs.text: The notion of consumer detriment in the digital context employed by the Commission in the "Fitness Check" is that of "a situation in which consumers experience negative outcomes when taking transactional decisions regarding different products and services online". This includes financial as well as non-financial harms. To assess individual financial consumer detriment, the Commission further measured

section_path: 2. Assessing Consumer Harm in a digital world
paragraphs.text: "revealed personal consumer detriment, which includes negative outcomes for individual consumers which they become aware of following the purchase or use of a product or

section_path: 2. Assessing Consumer Harm in a digital world
paragraphs.text: At this stage, the Call for Evidence invites stakeholders' views on the problems, objectives, and potential policy options. The next stage—the impact assessment—will require robust evidence to substantiate those views, assessing the effectiveness, costs, and distributional consequences of potential interventions. In what follows, we illustrate some of the empirical challenges of establishing a theory of harm and where relevant quantum of harm caused, based on the examples of drip pricing and potentially unfair contract terms, especially subscription cancellation practices. These examples are meant to illustrate the kind of analysis that can be helpful in coming to an informed view about the true costs of such practices and whether these indeed constitute "unfair" behaviour. The below examples are by no means meant to constitute an exhaustive list of the kind of economic issues that ought to be assessed to justify whether regulation is required and / or whether a specific practice is abusive.

section_path: 2. Assessing Consumer Harm in a digital world

section_path: 2. Assessing Consumer Harm in a digital world
paragraphs.text: Is drip pricing always "unfair"

section_path: 3. Is drip pricing always "unfair"
paragraphs.text: This section offers context and insights relating to the DFA as it may seek to address unfair practices related to the price (e.g. "drip" pricing, "starting from" prices if the trader applies dynamic pricing, percentage/value discounts that mislead the consumer as to the nature of the promotion);

section_path: 3.1. What is drip pricing?
paragraphs.text: A product's price is one of the most salient attributes that consumers consider in a market-based economy. Consumers rely on sellers competing with each other to attract them with the lowest price. The importance of price to consumers in choosing what to purchase also gives some sellers an incentive to make prices harder to evaluate and respond to by increasing the complexity of how a price is presented or otherwise obfuscating the price. "[price] obfuscation hurts consumers in two ways: consumers incur higher search costs and also pay higher prices."6

section_path: 3.1. What is drip pricing?
paragraphs.text: A relatively simple way to increase the complexity of pricing is to separate a total price into a base price and a separately presented "fee." Prices structured like this – where the information needed to calculate the total price is separated spatially but provided at the same time – are typically referred to as "partitioned" prices. For example, an advertisement that presents a 400 Euro offer price in large prominent font, and separately but available at the same time, notes an added 20 Euro fee, uses a partitioned price structure. Prices that are structured with a time delay between when pieces of the price are presented to, or made salient to, consumers are typically referred to as drip or "dripped" prices. For example, a sales process that focuses on negotiating a purported selling price and then later adds in a separate fixed fee uses dripped pricing.

section_path: 3.1. What is drip pricing?
paragraphs.text: Research on partitioned and dripped pricing has found that their use may increase the amount that consumers pay over unitary pricing.7 Functionally, partitioned and dripped pricing appear to change the way consumers perceive prices – i.e. their beliefs about prices – and those changes in perception then change the amount that consumers actually pay.

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: Charging consumers one or more separate fees in addition to a base price for a good or service is a common practice in consumer markets, and is not, by itself, harmful to consumers. For example:

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: a. A separate fee could truthfully signal that part of the total purchase price stems from some third party's independent influence on the price (e.g. government regulated fee or shipping charge), the existence of which could potentially inform the purchaser about what part of the price was likely to be similar across all sellers. Such a separate fee could also inform negotiations to the extent that it truthfully focuses on amounts under the seller's control.

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: © NERA

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: Is drip pricing always "unfair"

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: b. A separate fee could truthfully signal that some services or attributes, and thus some costs, are optional or avoidable. The choice presented to the buyer could include the consumer bypassing the services represented by the fee by choosing a different provider (for example, choosing to pick up a burrito at the restaurant rather than having it delivered by a service). This could also include the consumer making choices that avoid the costs represented by the fee (for example, refusing optional add-ons, or acting to avoid certain more expensive services).

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: In both of these examples, partitioned or dripped prices provide additional, useful information to consumers beyond what they would get from a unitary price. They do so by improving the consumer's understanding of the transaction, which then influences how the consumer approaches that transaction, either in negotiating price, choosing options, or choosing a competing product. That is, while there is a known possibility that a partitioned price will increase the price paid by consumers relative to a unitary price, that cost can in some cases be more than offset by the benefits of additional usable information to the consumer. Many fees provide useful information to consumers and thus, on net, make consumers better off by improving consumer understanding of prices and choices and thus allowing more beneficial choices.

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: However, a partitioned price that does NOT provide additional usable information to consumers simply increases the likelihood that consumers pay more, with no possibility of an offsetting benefit to consumers. Most clearly, a fixed, generally mandatory fee, separate from a base price that is not tied to any separable third-party attribute or consumer choice, does not provide any additional information that could benefit consumers. This is the most basic case of partitioned or dripped pricing, where a seller simply splits the total price into a part that is initially posted or negotiated, and which is separated by space and/or time from an additional fee, independent of any value, options or choices by the customer. Such fees are facially "junk" as they are useless to consumers and only create costs to consumers stemming from how they manipulate consumer understanding of total price and increase search costs.

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: NERA's analysis of market conditions and consumer behaviour in cases involving allegations of harmful pricing practices has shown that context matters in assessing possible harm. Issues that should be assessed include:

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: a. How difficult is it for consumers to observe all aspects of the price, and if desired back out of a transaction after that point?

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: Some prices – for example delivery fees that are contingent on the volume of an overall purchase may only be available after a customer completes their product selections. NERA analysis has shown that consumers readily change their purchase decisions, including adding or subtracting products, or abandoning the purchase entirely, after observing all aspects of the price. This demonstrates that the fee structure imposes little or no cost on consumers' ability to assess price that would counter the benefits of providing consumers those contingent, channel specific prices separately from base menu prices.

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: b. Can consumers influence or avoid the separated parts of the price?

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: The EU questionnaire on drip pricing specifically calls out fees that are mandatory and unavoidable, and we agree that this should be part of the assessment of a fee. As noted above, price information is key to consumer choice and providing a separate fee can inform

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: © NERA

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: Is drip pricing always "unfair"

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: consumers about the cost of various options within a platform, as well as the cost of using a particular platform relative to another. That is, highlighting the costs of various aspects of a transaction provides significant value to consumers in choosing a market platform through which to make purchases, both by flagging the added charges from that platform and by providing information on the likely price of the base goods on other platforms. In our view this means that the application of mandatory and unavoidable should be applied on a market wide basis, not just on a platform basis.

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: These issues mean that novel or emerging pricing practices, including partitioned or dripped pricing, are often not harmful to consumers – even when they may increase the complexity of the price or invoke concerns of decreased price sensitivity – because of the value of the information that they provide compared to the relatively low costs to consumers of navigating the extra complexity.

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: The DFA should take into account such nuance in considering i) whether banning any such practices outright could be justified and ii) when providing regulators with the tools to assess whether specific conduct that's not outright banned rises to the level of sanctionable "unfairness".

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: © NERA

section_path: 3.2. Is charging a base price plus a fee harmful to consumers?
paragraphs.text: Preventing unfair contract terms in subscriptions

section_path: 4. Preventing unfair contract terms in subscriptions
paragraphs.text: Another focus of the proposed DFA is the marketing / design of subscriptions, notably regarding the cancellation or renewal of subscriptions or the conversion of free trials into paid subscriptions.

section_path: 4. Preventing unfair contract terms in subscriptions
paragraphs.text: Drawing on a combined 17 years of experience at the U.S. Federal Trade Commission (FTC) that coincided with the first decade of enforcement of the Restore Online Shoppers' Confidence Act (ROSCA), several key observations and lessons emerge that could enhance the regulatory framework of the DFA.

section_path: 4. Preventing unfair contract terms in subscriptions
paragraphs.text: ROSCA enforcement centered on 3 main issues, which overlap well with the concerns about subscriptions identified in the Digital Fairness Act Fitness Check: disclosure of material terms prior to obtaining billing information; express, informed consent before charging a consumer's account; and simple mechanisms for cancellation. For each issue, we will identify data and research that would prove invaluable in addressing unresolved policy questions we have encountered.

section_path: 4.1. Disclosure of material terms
paragraphs.text: A hallmark of FTC ROSCA enforcement has been its frequent invocation of the provision involving disclosure of material terms. On its face, requiring the disclosure of material terms prior to billing seems innocuous. Still, after a decade of enforcement, a policy statement, and a recent rulemaking important policy questions remain and rigorous research is needed for the DFA to strike the right balance of costs and benefits to consumers from potentially introducing these kinds of mandatory disclosures.

section_path: 4.1. Disclosure of material terms
Are misleading or deceptive claims more or less prevalent in subscription marketing?
paragraphs.text: Research on the incidence of such claims in both subscription marketing and traditional one-time transaction marketing will be invaluable for informing whether stricter regulatory treatment (relative to baseline prohibitions on deceptive advertising) is even warranted.

section_path: 4.1. Disclosure of material terms
Are misleading or deceptive claims more likely to cause harm in subscription marketing?
paragraphs.text: Due to the ongoing nature of the products at issue, product characteristics are much more likely to change over time, so what is misleading today may not be misleading tomorrow. In addition, subscription products are more likely to have free trial periods, which may obviate the need for extensive disclosures as there is no substitute for experience with the product. In assessing whether an arrangement is "unfair", (potentially case-specific) analysis can be undertaken to assess whether consumers feel more informed about a product after lengthy disclosures or after a free trial period.

section_path: 4.1. Disclosure of material terms
How frequently is material information contingent on customer billing information?
paragraphs.text: Fees, shipping charges, and taxes can depend on the consumer's location. The amount and timing of charges may also depend on the mechanism of payment (credit card, debit card, bank transfer

section_path: 4.1. Disclosure of material terms
How frequently is material information contingent on customer billing information?
paragraphs.text: © NERA

section_path: 4.1. Disclosure of material terms

paragraphs.text: Preventing unfair contract terms in subscriptions

section_path: 4.1. Disclosure of material terms
How frequently is material information contingent on customer billing information?
paragraphs.text: etc.). Collecting data on the frequency of these exceptions will inform whether the DFA should have explicit carve-outs for specific types of material information.

section_path: 4.2. Express, informed consent
paragraphs.text: Several recent and high-profile FTC investigations have alleged that customers are frequently signed-up for subscription products without their knowledge or consent.[12] It is also fairly clear from existing research that the potential for consumer confusion is high in "one-click" environments, and the consequences of that confusion can be significant when recurring charges are involved.[13] Still, there is a fundamental trade-off between reducing consumer friction and increasing the potential for confusion and mistakes that must be explored through research / take account of the specifics of a certain case at hand in order to strike the correct balance.

section_path: 4.2. Express, informed consent
paragraphs.text: How little friction is too little? How much is too much? Obviously, subscription products exist because consumers find it valuable to not have to deliberately engage with the company periodically just because they have a predictable need or want to maintain continuous access to a service. On the other hand, virtually all consumer mistakes could be eliminated by introducing friction to transactions through additional confirmations. Even though each interaction is not very costly, making $100\%$ of users spend the time to engage in multiple confirmations has the potential to be more costly in a welfare sense than allowing some fraction of users to make mistakes. Consumer research can be conducted to evaluate how much consumers value this convenience in order to weigh this against the cost of unintentional transactions.

section_path: 4.2. Express, informed consent
paragraphs.text: How far would a confirmation notice sent during a grace period with a prominent option to cancel go towards mitigating the harm from inadvertent transactions? Instead of imposing small costs on everyone in order to avoid mistakes, the flip side of the approach above would be to reduce friction upfront, but then to allow for the easy unwinding of mistakes for those fraction of consumers who commit them. Which approach is superior depends on how likely mistakes are to occur and the costliness of mistakes if they go unremedied, which can only be determined via empirical research.

section_path: 4.3. Simple mechanisms for cancellation
paragraphs.text: The third issue that has become a centerpiece of ROSCA enforcement is the ease with which consumers can stop recurring charges. Almost all recent FTC investigations involving ROSCA have included an allegation that the cancellation mechanisms were overly complex or cumbersome.[14] However, the plain meaning of "simplicity" is subjective. The FTC, in its policy guidance, has set the bar for simplicity of cancellation in relation to the simplicity of the sign-up process.[15] But even if that were the correct benchmark from a welfare perspective (consider whether companies now have perverse incentives to make sign-up more difficult), it's not clear that companies have

section_path: 4.3. Simple mechanisms for cancellation
paragraphs.text: © NERA

section_path: 4.3. Simple mechanisms for cancellation
paragraphs.text: Preventing unfair contract terms in subscriptions

section_path: 4.3. Simple mechanisms for cancellation
paragraphs.text: sufficient information to adequately comply. We believe that directed research could easily address some of these concerns in order to set sensible "rules of the road" when it comes to subscription and subscription cancellation.

section_path: How long does it take to sign up for and cancel subscriptions? And what are consumers' expectations of same?
paragraphs.text: To our knowledge, there has been no rigorous and comprehensive evaluation of the time it takes to sign-up for and cancel various subscriptions. In fact, in their recent rulemaking the FTC relied upon an article by a journalist in the popular press to estimate typical cancellation times for online subscriptions. [16] Regulatory authorities should conduct a rigorous and comprehensive study of the time it takes consumers to sign-up for and cancel subscriptions. It should also cover what consumers expect when it comes to the time spent signing up for and cancelling subscriptions. This study would establish vital context for what "simple" means that is sensitive to the context of the consumer transaction, including the industry, channel (e.g., in-person, by phone, over the internet, etc.), size of the transaction, length of billing cycle, etc. To date, companies have been operating in a vacuum with respect to what constitutes a typical or reasonable time to spend in a sign-up or cancellation flow. A definitive study conducted by an authoritative source would resolve a great deal of uncertainty and give companies a yardstick with which to compare themselves.

section_path: Do "save offers" unnecessarily complicate cancellation, or are they valuable to consumers on balance?
paragraphs.text: "Save offers", i.e. companies offering would-be leaving customers a revised deal when threatening to cancel can be one reason why leaving a subscription takes longer than signing up. The FTC's policy guidance is that such offers are a violation of ROSCA when they "impose unreasonable delays on consumers' cancellation efforts." But it is also clear that consumers leverage cancellation of subscriptions in order to get better offers from companies. Research should investigate whether there is a true welfare trade-off here by examining whether "save offers" are equally as effective when made immediately after the termination of the subscription contract, as opposed to immediately prior. This change would allow companies to companies to avoid unreasonable delays for individuals motivated to cancel, while still allowing them to "retain" (after a negligible lapse) customers who are shopping for a discount. If so, companies can make cancellation simple while still retaining an important tool.

section_path: Is a consumer's failure to cancel a subscription due to the complexity of the process, or to a lack of motivation?
paragraphs.text: Evidence of cancellation complexity often comes in the form of empirical evidence documenting how many consumers fail to complete a cancellation flow once initiated. This connection is intuitive, as it's easy to envision a consumer being stymied by a complicated cancellation process and being unable to navigate their way to the end. At the same time, a typical cancellation flow can also reveal information to the consumer about the value of the subscription. Additionally, if the cost of initiating the cancellation process is low (one-click on an app or website), it is not a very strong signal of the consumer's motivation to cancel in the first place. Failing to complete the flow could merely be a consequence of the consumer's ambivalence. As a result, observational data like this conflates the objective difficulty of the process with a consumer's motivation to complete it. Cancellation complexity needs to be evaluated in a controlled setting, such as a laboratory experiment, where individuals are clearly incentivised to

section_path: Is a consumer's failure to cancel a subscription due to the complexity of the process, or to a lack of motivation?
paragraphs.text: [16] See "Negative Option Rule" (89 FR 90476), footnote 544.

paragraphs.text: cancel. Only then can the complexity of a cancellation flow be evaluated independently of the consumer's underlying preference for the product.

section_path: Is a consumer's failure to cancel a subscription due to the complexity of the process, or to a lack of motivation?
paragraphs.text: The insights derived from the enforcement of ROSCA highlight the complexities inherent in regulating subscription marketing practices. As the DFA moves towards potential enactment, incorporating these lessons can help shape a robust regulatory framework that prioritises consumer protection, transparency, and fairness. By addressing key questions regarding disclosures, consent, and cancellation mechanisms, the DFA can become a pivotal step in ensuring that the digital economy operates in a manner that aligns with consumer interests.

section_path: Is a consumer's failure to cancel a subscription due to the complexity of the process, or to a lack of motivation?

paragraphs.text: About the main authors

section_path: 5. About the main authors
paragraphs.text: Dominik Huebler is a Director in NERA's Berlin office. He specialises in regulatory economics covering amongst other things data privacy and access issues as well as issued related to the regulation of energy, communications and transport markets. He led NERA's work in one of the first GDPR privacy damages cases to reach the European Court of Justice as well as NERA's work on access terms and prices for vehicle data. He has also represented clients accused of excessive / unfair pricing in various energy markets. Prior to joining NERA in 2008, he obtained his graduate degree in economics from the University of Oxford.

section_path: 5. About the main authors
paragraphs.text: He can be reached at Dominik.Huebler@nera.com.

section_path: 5. About the main authors
paragraphs.text: Mike LeGower, PhD is a Director in NERA's Washington DC office. He has more than 10 years of experience in investigations and litigation consulting, specializing in consumer protection and competition issues. He served as an economist and, ultimately, Deputy Assistant Director in the U.S. Federal Trade Commission's Bureau of Economics from 2014 to 2025, where he oversaw the economic analysis of cases involving deceptive marketing and unfair business practices. Dr. LeGower has experience evaluating scientific and statistical evidence in support of advertising as well as conducting independent and agency-sponsored economic research for publications.

section_path: 5. About the main authors
paragraphs.text: He can be reached at Mike.LeGower@nera.com.

section_path: 5. About the main authors
paragraphs.text: Andrew Stivers is a Managing Director in NERA's Washington DC office. He specializes in the economics of consumer protection and privacy. He has developed and conducted analysis of novel and complex questions of consumer behavior and injury related to privacy, algorithmically driven practices, and cutting-edge promotion and advertising strategies. Prior to joining NERA Dr. Stivers led the consumer protection economics work at the U.S. Federal Trade Commission from 2014 to 2021.

section_path: 5. About the main authors
paragraphs.text: He can be reached at Andrew.Stivers@nera.com.

section_path: QUALIFICATIONS, ASSUMPTIONS, AND LIMITING CONDITIONS
paragraphs.text: NERA shall not have any liability to any third party in respect of this report or any actions taken or decisions made as a consequence of the results, advice or recommendations set forth herein. This report does not represent investment advice or provide an opinion regarding the fairness of any transaction to any and all parties. This report does not represent legal advice, which can only be provided by legal counsel and for which you should seek advice of counsel. The opinions expressed herein are valid only for the purpose stated herein and as of the date thereof. Information furnished by others, upon which all or portions of this report are based, is believed to be reliable but has not been verified.
